Rule Name: In-App Purchase Required for Digital Goods
Keywords: iap, in-app purchase, payment, digital goods, subscription, storekit, apple pay, commission
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 3.1.1
Requirement: All digital goods and subscriptions sold within the app must use Apple's In-App Purchase (StoreKit). External payment links are prohibited for digital content. Physical goods and services (Uber, Airbnb) are exempt.
Impact If Violated: App rejection. Apple has rejected major apps (Epic Games, Spotify) for violating this rule. Appeals are lengthy and rarely succeed.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: IAP for all digital content purchases. Factor Apple's 30% (or 15% for small business) commission into pricing model. Include 'Restore Purchases' button on paywall screen.
Common Mistakes: Trying to use Stripe/PayPal for digital subscriptions. Linking to a website for payment. Not including 'Restore Purchases' button.

Keywords: google play billing, in-app purchase, subscription, digital goods, payment, android monetization
Guideline Reference: Google Play Billing Policy
Requirement: Apps distributed on Google Play that offer digital content must use Google Play's billing system. Google allows some alternative billing (user choice billing in select markets) but still requires Play Billing as an option.
Impact If Violated: App removal from Play Store. Account warnings. Potential developer account suspension for repeated violations.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: Google Play Billing for digital subscriptions. Factor Google's 15-30% commission into pricing. Support both Google Play Billing and any alternative billing if using user choice billing.
Common Mistakes: Assuming Google's billing rules are identical to Apple's. Not supporting Google Play Billing alongside alternative payment.

Keywords: privacy, nutrition label, data collection, app privacy, tracking, transparency, privacy policy
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 5.1.1 + App Privacy Details
Requirement: Every app must declare what data it collects, how it's used, and whether it's linked to the user's identity. This is shown on the App Store listing page before download.
Impact If Violated: App rejection if declared data doesn't match actual collection. User trust damage if the label is inaccurate. Apple has removed apps for false privacy declarations.
PRD Action Required: PRD must include a 'Data Collection' section listing every data point collected, its purpose, and whether it's linked to identity. Review with engineering before submission. Update the label with every feature that adds data collection.
Common Mistakes: Not updating the label when adding analytics SDKs. Forgetting that third-party SDKs (Facebook, Google Analytics) also collect data that must be declared.

Keywords: data safety, privacy, data collection, google play, transparency, data handling
Guideline Reference: Google Play Data Safety Section Policy
Requirement: Every app must complete a Data Safety form declaring data collection, sharing, and security practices. Shown on the Play Store listing page.
Impact If Violated: App flagged or removed. Play Store shows warning badge if Data Safety section is incomplete. Users increasingly check this before installing.
PRD Action Required: PRD must include data collection inventory. Map every data point to its purpose and retention policy. Update Data Safety form with every feature release that changes data handling.
Common Mistakes: Not updating the form when adding new third-party SDKs. Declaring 'no data shared' when analytics SDKs share data with their servers.

Keywords: att, tracking, idfa, advertising, attribution, app tracking transparency, privacy prompt, ios 14
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 5.1.2 (ATT Framework)
Requirement: Apps must use the ATT framework to request permission before tracking users across apps/websites owned by other companies. The user must explicitly opt in via a system dialog.
Impact If Violated: App rejection. IDFA access revoked without prompt. Advertising attribution accuracy drops significantly without opt-in.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: ATT prompt timing and copy (custom message string). Expected opt-in rate (typically 15-35% globally). Impact on advertising attribution and ROAS measurement. Fallback attribution strategy for users who deny.
Common Mistakes: Prompting ATT on first launch (kills opt-in rate). Not having a fallback attribution strategy. Assuming all users will opt in.

Keywords: background location, always location, gps, tracking, geofence, significant location change
Guideline Reference: Apple: HIG Location Best Practices. Google: Background Location Access Policy
Requirement: Background location ('Always') requires clear justification. iOS: App Store review requires a written explanation of why background location is needed. Android: Separate permission prompt for background location. Both platforms may reject if the use case doesn't justify it.
Impact If Violated: iOS: App rejection. Android: Separate permission dialog that most users deny. Both: User complaints about battery drain. App store reviews citing 'tracking.'
PRD Action Required: PRD must justify background location need. Specify: use case (navigation, geofencing, fitness tracking), minimum accuracy needed, frequency of location updates, battery impact assessment. Default to 'While Using' unless background is essential.
Common Mistakes: Requesting 'Always' when 'While Using' is sufficient. Not explaining the justification in App Store submission notes. Ignoring battery impact of continuous GPS.

Keywords: minimum functionality, utility, wrapper, web view, thin app, app store quality, content
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 4.2
Requirement: Apps must provide sufficient functionality beyond a repackaged website. Web view wrappers, apps with minimal content, and apps that are 'too simple' are rejected. Apps must work without an internet connection if possible.
Impact If Violated: App rejection with guidance to submit as a website instead. Common reason for first-time app rejections.
PRD Action Required: PRD must demonstrate native value: features that leverage device capabilities (camera, sensors, push, offline), performance advantages over web, or UX patterns that justify a native app. Include at least 3 features that are only possible or significantly better as a native app.
Common Mistakes: Building a web view wrapper and submitting it as an app. Not implementing any offline functionality. Not using any native device capabilities.

Keywords: moderation, ugc, user generated content, report, abuse, safety, harmful content, csam
Guideline Reference: Apple: 1.2 User Generated Content. Google: User Generated Content Policy
Requirement: Apps with user-generated content must implement: content reporting mechanism, blocking functionality, content moderation (human and/or automated), and the ability to hide objectionable content. Apps with UGC aimed at children have stricter requirements.
Impact If Violated: App rejection or removal. Legal liability for hosting harmful content. Regulatory fines (DSA in EU, COPPA for children's content).
PRD Action Required: PRD must include: reporting flow (how users flag content), moderation pipeline (automated filters + human review), blocking mechanism (user-to-user), content policy documentation accessible in-app, response time SLA for reported content (typically <24h).
Common Mistakes: Launching UGC features without moderation. Relying solely on automated moderation without human review. Not having a content policy accessible in the app.

Keywords: login wall, account requirement, sign in, guest access, anonymous, browse, explore
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 5.1.1(v)
Requirement: Apps must justify mandatory login. Apple expects users to be able to explore the app's core functionality before being required to create an account. Apps that force login before showing any value are frequently rejected.
Impact If Violated: App rejection. Apple specifically flags apps that gate content behind login without justification.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: which screens are accessible without login (minimum: core browsing/discovery), at which point login is required (first write action or personalized feature), guest mode behavior. Include a 'Login Strategy' note in onboarding section.
Common Mistakes: Requiring signup/login on the first screen. Not providing any guest or anonymous browsing capability. Requiring social login only (must offer email/phone alternative).

Keywords: sign in with apple, social login, apple id, authentication, third party login
Guideline Reference: App Store Review Guideline 4.8
Requirement: Apps that offer third-party sign-in (Google, Facebook, etc.) must also offer Sign in with Apple as an option. It must be presented as an equally prominent option alongside other social login buttons.
Impact If Violated: App rejection. This is a hard requirement — no exceptions. Apple enforces it strictly.
PRD Action Required: PRD must include: Sign in with Apple button on login/signup screen, equal prominence with other social login options, handling of Apple's email relay (private email forwarding), handling of 'Hide My Email' users.
Common Mistakes: Not implementing Sign in with Apple when offering Google/Facebook login. Placing the Apple button less prominently than other options. Not handling the private relay email properly.

Keywords: push, notification, promotional, marketing, transactional, notification abuse, engagement
Guideline Reference: Apple: HIG Notifications. Google: FCM Best Practices
Requirement: Push notifications must provide value to the user. Excessive promotional notifications, notifications used solely for advertising, or notifications that trick users into opening the app are grounds for rejection (iOS) or policy violation (Android).
Impact If Violated: iOS: App rejection. Both: Users disable notifications or uninstall. App store reviews citing 'spam.'
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: notification categories (transactional vs promotional), frequency caps per category, user control (per-category toggles), quiet hours support. Never send promotional push without explicit user opt-in for that category.
Common Mistakes: Using push for pure advertising. Not providing per-category notification controls. Sending notifications during sleeping hours. No frequency caps.

Keywords: accessibility, a11y, voiceover, talkback, wcag, dynamic type, font size, color contrast, assistive
Guideline Reference: Apple: HIG Accessibility. Google: Material Accessibility. WCAG 2.1 AA
Requirement: Apps should support: VoiceOver (iOS) / TalkBack (Android) screen readers, Dynamic Type (iOS) / font scaling (Android), sufficient color contrast (4.5:1 for text), touch targets ≥44x44pt. While not a hard rejection criterion, Apple features apps with good accessibility.
Impact If Violated: Missed App Store featuring opportunity. Legal liability (ADA lawsuits for mobile apps are increasing). Exclusion of 15% of global population with disabilities.
PRD Action Required: PRD must include: accessibility section with VoiceOver/TalkBack testing plan, Dynamic Type support for all text, color contrast compliance (4.5:1), minimum touch targets (44x44pt iOS, 48x48dp Android), meaningful labels for all interactive elements.
Common Mistakes: Treating accessibility as post-launch. Not testing with screen readers. Using color as the only indicator of state. Making touch targets too small.

Keywords: app size, download size, binary size, cellular download, app thinning, aab, bundle
Guideline Reference: Apple: 200MB cellular download limit. Google: recommended <150MB (AAB)
Requirement: iOS: Apps >200MB cannot be downloaded over cellular without user confirmation. Android: Large apps have lower install rates. Both platforms recommend keeping apps as small as possible for conversion.
Impact If Violated: Reduced install conversion rate (every 10MB increase costs ~1% installs in emerging markets). iOS cellular download warning reduces impulse installs.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: target app download size. If >100MB, document why and plan for reduction. Include app size as a non-functional requirement. Plan for asset optimization (image compression, code splitting, on-demand resources).
Common Mistakes: Including high-resolution assets for all device sizes. Bundling unused libraries. Not using App Thinning (iOS) or Android App Bundles.

Keywords: screenshot, preview, app store listing, aso, store page, marketing, video preview
Guideline Reference: Apple: App Store Product Page Guidelines. Google: Store Listing Requirements
Requirement: App store screenshots must accurately represent the app. Misleading screenshots or previews are grounds for rejection. Screenshots must show actual app UI (not conceptual mockups).
Impact If Violated: App rejection. User complaints of misleading listings. Negative reviews citing 'doesn't match screenshots.'
PRD Action Required: PRD for launch must include: screenshot plan (5-10 screens showing key features), preview video spec (15-30s, shows actual UI), localization plan for screenshots in target markets. Screenshots should be finalized after UI is feature-complete, not during design.
Common Mistakes: Using pre-design mockups as final screenshots. Not updating screenshots when UI changes significantly. Not localizing screenshots for non-English markets.

Keywords: children, kids, coppa, family, age gate, parental consent, education, child safety
Guideline Reference: Apple: Kids Category Requirements. Google: Families Policy
Requirement: Apps targeting children have strict requirements: no behavioral advertising, parental consent for data collection (COPPA), no external links without parental gate, age-appropriate content only. Apps in Kids category have additional restrictions.
Impact If Violated: App removal. FTC fines for COPPA violations (fines can be millions). Reputational damage.
PRD Action Required: If targeting children: PRD must include age gate mechanism, parental consent flow, compliant advertising approach (no behavioral ads), data minimization plan. State clearly whether the app targets children in the App Store Compliance section.
Common Mistakes: Not implementing age verification. Using standard ad SDKs that track children. Collecting data without parental consent. Including external links without parental gate.

Keywords: subscription, cancel, manage, auto-renew, billing, refund, subscription management
Guideline Reference: Apple: 3.1.2 Subscriptions. Google: Subscriptions Policy
Requirement: Apps must clearly communicate: subscription price, billing period, auto-renewal terms, and cancellation instructions. iOS: must link to subscription management. Both: must honor cancellations and not charge after cancellation.
Impact If Violated: App rejection. User complaints. Regulatory fines (FTC has fined companies for dark pattern subscription practices).
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: subscription terms displayed before purchase, easy cancellation path (Settings > Manage Subscription link), grace period handling (3-day on iOS, configurable on Android), billing retry for failed payments, clear communication of price changes.
Common Mistakes: Hiding cancellation flow. Not communicating auto-renewal terms. Not handling billing failures gracefully. Not supporting subscription upgrades/downgrades.

Rule Name: Deep Link and Universal Link Requirements
Keywords: deep link, universal link, app link, associated domains, intent filter, url handling
Guideline Reference: Apple: Universal Links. Google: Android App Links
Requirement: Universal Links (iOS) and App Links (Android) require server-side configuration (apple-app-site-association / assetlinks.json). Both platforms prioritize verified deep links over custom URL schemes.
Impact If Violated: Broken deep links lead to web fallback instead of app opening. User confusion when links don't work. Reduced conversion from external channels (email, social, ads).
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: all deep link paths and their in-app destinations. Server-side configuration requirements (AASA file for iOS, assetlinks.json for Android). Fallback behavior for each link when app is not installed. Testing plan for both platforms.
Common Mistakes: Using custom URL schemes instead of Universal Links / App Links. Not configuring server-side verification files. Not handling the case where app is not installed (deferred deep link).

Keywords: biometric, face id, touch id, fingerprint, local authentication, keychain, keystore, secure enclave
Guideline Reference: Apple: LocalAuthentication Framework. Google: BiometricPrompt API
Requirement: Biometric data must never leave the device. Use platform APIs (LocalAuthentication on iOS, BiometricPrompt on Android). Always provide a non-biometric fallback (PIN, password). Don't store biometric data — store authentication tokens in Keychain/Keystore.
Impact If Violated: Security vulnerabilities if biometric data is mishandled. App rejection if using non-standard biometric APIs. User trust damage.
PRD Action Required: PRD must specify: which actions require biometric auth, fallback authentication method, session timeout before re-auth, secure storage mechanism (Keychain/Keystore), behavior when biometric hardware is not available.
Common Mistakes: Storing biometric data in app storage. Not providing a fallback for devices without biometric hardware. Using third-party biometric libraries instead of platform APIs.

Keywords: widget, home screen, lock screen, live activity, app extension, glance, complication
Guideline Reference: Apple: WidgetKit Guidelines. Google: App Widgets Guidelines
Requirement: Widgets must be lightweight, load quickly, and provide glanceable information. iOS: WidgetKit is the only supported framework (no web views in widgets). Android: RemoteViews with size limits. Both: widgets should not require authentication to display useful information.
Impact If Violated: Poor widget performance = negative app perception. iOS: Widgets that fail to load show a blank placeholder. Android: Widgets that crash may be removed by the system.
PRD Action Required: PRD for widget features must specify: widget sizes supported, content refresh frequency, data source (cached vs API), behavior when user is not logged in, behavior when offline. Include widget as a separate user story, not bundled with the main feature.
Common Mistakes: Building overly complex widgets. Requiring login to show any widget content. Not handling offline state. Refreshing too frequently (kills battery).

Keywords: app review, submission, review notes, demo account, test flight, review time, metadata
Guideline Reference: Apple: App Review Guidelines (General)
Requirement: Provide demo credentials for reviewers, include detailed review notes explaining new features, ensure all features are testable without special hardware or accounts. Average review time is 24-48h but can be longer for complex apps.
Impact If Violated: Extended review times. Rejection for untestable features. Delayed launches. Having to prepare emergency resubmission materials.
PRD Action Required: PRD launch section must include: app review preparation checklist, demo account credentials for reviewers, review notes template explaining each new feature, list of features that require special setup to test, timeline buffer (3-5 days) for review + potential rejection + resubmission.
Common Mistakes: Not preparing demo credentials. Not writing review notes. Not accounting for review time in launch timeline. Not preparing for potential rejection and resubmission cycle.
